seoTitle: Allegro MicroSystems Solutions | EV Battery Management, Motor Control | BeiLuo
seoDescription: Explore Allegro MicroSystems application solutions for electric vehicles, industrial automation, and motor control. Complete reference designs and technical support.

faqs.question: What application solutions does Allegro offer?
faqs.answer: Allegro MicroSystems provides comprehensive application solutions across key markets: 1) Electric Vehicle Battery Management - high-current sensors for pack monitoring, contactor control, and thermal management; 2) Motor Control Systems - BLDC, stepper, and brushed DC motor drivers for automotive and industrial applications; 3) ADAS and Safety Systems - magnetic position sensors for steering, braking, and transmission; 4) Industrial Automation - current sensors for motor drives, solar inverters, and power supplies; 5) LED Lighting - automotive and industrial LED drivers with advanced thermal management; 6) Power Conversion - current sensing for switch-mode power supplies and DC-DC converters. Each solution includes reference designs, evaluation boards, and application-specific technical documentation to accelerate development.
faqs.decisionGuide: Browse our solutions by application area or contact our FAE team for solution recommendations.

faqs.question: How do Allegro solutions help with EV battery management?
faqs.answer: Allegro's sensing solutions address critical EV battery management needs: High-voltage isolation - ACS37610 provides 4.8kV isolation for 400V/800V battery packs; High-current measurement - ±200A range monitors fast charging and discharge currents; Fast fault detection - < 1μs response for short-circuit protection; Temperature monitoring - integrated temperature sensors track cell and pack temperatures; Contact position sensing - magnetic sensors monitor high-voltage contactor status; Current imbalance detection - differential sensing identifies cell imbalance conditions. The solutions meet automotive safety standards (ISO 26262) with diagnostic features and redundant protection. Reference designs include complete BMS architectures with Allegro sensors, gate drivers, and interface circuits.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use ACS37610 for high-current pack monitoring; implement fast OCD for protection; add magnetic sensors for contactor position.

faqs.question: What motor control solutions does Allegro provide?
faqs.answer: Allegro offers complete motor control solutions: BLDC Motor Control - AMT49105 integrated driver with sensorless control, sinusoidal commutation, and FOC support; Stepper Motor Control - A4988/DRV8825 with microstepping and current regulation; Brushed DC Control - A4950 full-bridge driver with current sensing; Position Feedback - A1335 angle sensors for servo control, A1324 linear sensors for position control; Current Sensing - ACS712/ACS720 for torque control and overcurrent protection. Solutions include: Complete reference designs with schematics and PCB layouts; Firmware libraries for sensorless control; Evaluation kits with motors and software; Application notes for motor tuning and optimization. Automotive-qualified options available for electric pumps, cooling fans, and power steering applications.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use AMT49105 for automotive BLDC; A4988 for stepper control; A4950 for brushed DC; add angle sensors for position feedback.

solutions.name: Electric Vehicle Battery Management System
solutions.description: Complete current sensing solution for EV/HEV battery pack monitoring with high-voltage isolation and fast fault protection.
solutions.longDescription: Allegro's EV Battery Management solution provides comprehensive current sensing for electric vehicle battery packs. The system uses ACS37610 high-current sensors with 4.8kV isolation to safely monitor pack currents up to ±200A in 400V/800V systems. Fast overcurrent detection (< 1μs) provides critical protection during short-circuit conditions, while high bandwidth (1MHz) enables precise monitoring of fast charging and regenerative braking currents. The differential Hall sensing architecture rejects external magnetic fields from adjacent power cables, ensuring accurate measurements in the noisy EV environment. Integrated temperature compensation maintains ±2% accuracy across the automotive temperature range (-40°C to 150°C).
features: 4.8kV galvanic isolation for HV battery packs
benefits: Safe high-voltage measurement with integrated isolation
Fast protection prevents battery damage during faults
Accurate current measurement for SOC/SOH estimation
Robust operation in noisy automotive environments
Reduced BOM cost vs discrete sensor + isolation solutions

customerCases.problem: Existing shunt-based sensing required external isolation amplifiers, increasing BOM cost and complexity
customerCases.diagnosis: Discrete solution with shunt + isolation amp had higher cost, larger size, and lower bandwidth
customerCases.solution: Redesigned with ACS37610 integrated sensors providing isolation and sensing in one package
customerCases.results: Reduced BOM cost by 40%, PCB area by 50%, improved bandwidth from 50kHz to 1MHz

faeInsights.insight: In my experience with EV battery management designs, the ACS37610's integrated isolation is a game-changer. Traditional shunt + isolation amplifier solutions require multiple components and careful layout to maintain isolation creepage distances. The ACS37610 integrates everything in one package, simplifying design and improving reliability. The 1MHz bandwidth is essential for modern SiC inverters - lower bandwidth sensors miss critical switching information. I always recommend implementing both the analog output for continuous monitoring and the OCD output for fast protection. The OCD can directly shut down gate drivers in < 1μs, preventing damage during short circuits. For thermal design, ensure adequate copper area or use the busbar itself as a heatsink.
faeInsights.logic: EV BMS design priorities: 1) Safety - high isolation voltage essential; 2) Protection - fast fault detection prevents damage; 3) Accuracy - precise current for SOC estimation; 4) Reliability - automotive qualification required. Allegro's integrated sensors address all four priorities better than discrete solutions.
keyTakeaways: Integrated isolation simplifies design and improves safety
1MHz bandwidth essential for SiC inverter monitoring
Dual analog + OCD outputs provide monitoring and protection
Proper thermal design critical for high-current operation
Differential sensing provides noise immunity in EV environment
commonPitfalls: Inadequate thermal design causing temperature drift
Ignoring external magnetic fields from adjacent cables
Insufficient isolation distance in PCB layout
Slow protection response allowing device damage
Poor filtering causing noise in measurements
bestPractices: Use busbar mounting for best thermal performance
Implement both analog monitoring and OCD protection
Route differential traces as twisted pair
Add RC filtering at ADC input for noise reduction
Calibrate offset at system level for best accuracy
troubleshootingTips: Check thermal performance with IR camera at max current
Verify isolation integrity with hipot tester
Measure OCD response time with oscilloscope
Check for external field interference with gaussmeter
Validate accuracy with precision current source

faqs.question: What isolation rating is needed for 800V EV systems?
faqs.answer: For 800V EV battery systems, a minimum of 2.5kV working voltage isolation is recommended, with 4.8kV providing additional safety margin. The ACS37610's 4.8kV isolation rating supports reinforced insulation requirements for touch-safe designs. According to IEC 60664, working voltage for basic insulation at 800V requires 1.6kV test voltage, while reinforced insulation requires 3.2kV. The ACS37610's 4.8kV rating exceeds both requirements. For compliance with UL 2580 (EV battery safety), the isolation must withstand 2kV + 2× working voltage (3.6kV for 800V systems). The 4.8kV rating provides margin for this requirement. Always verify specific safety certifications for your target markets.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use 4.8kV isolation for 800V systems; ensures compliance with UL 2580 and IEC 60664; provides safety margin.

faqs.question: How fast can the overcurrent protection respond?
faqs.answer: The ACS37610 overcurrent detection (OCD) responds in < 1μs from current exceeding threshold to OCD output going low. This fast response enables protection of IGBTs and MOSFETs during short-circuit conditions. Typical IGBT short-circuit withstand time is 10μs, so the < 1μs detection leaves 9μs for gate driver shutdown. System-level response time includes: Sensor OCD response (< 1μs) + Gate driver propagation delay (0.5-1μs) + MOSFET turn-off time (0.5-2μs) = Total < 4μs. This is well within device limits. For comparison, microcontroller-based protection typically takes 10-100μs, too slow for effective protection. The OCD output can directly drive gate driver shutdown pins or microcontroller interrupt inputs for fastest response.
faqs.decisionGuide: < 1μs OCD response enables effective short-circuit protection; direct gate driver shutdown recommended; much faster than MCU-based protection.

faqs.question: What accuracy is needed for EV battery SOC estimation?
faqs.answer: EV battery State of Charge (SOC) estimation typically requires current measurement accuracy of ±0.5% to ±1% for good performance. Coulomb counting (integrating current over time) accumulates error, so high accuracy is essential. The ACS37610 provides ±1% accuracy at 25°C and ±2% over temperature. For best SOC estimation: Use highest accuracy sensor (ACS37610 preferred over ACS712); Implement temperature compensation; Perform regular calibration (e.g., at full charge); Use Kalman filter or similar to combine current integration with voltage-based estimation. With ±1% current accuracy and proper algorithms, SOC estimation accuracy of ±2-3% is achievable. Lower accuracy sensors (±2-3%) result in SOC drift of ±5-10% over time, requiring more frequent recalibration.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use ±1% accuracy sensors for SOC estimation; implement temperature compensation; combine coulomb counting with voltage estimation.

faqs.question: How do I handle thermal management for high-current sensors?
faqs.answer: Thermal management for high-current sensors involves: Power dissipation calculation - P = I² × R_conductor. At 200A with 0.1mΩ: P = 4W. Thermal resistance - θja depends on mounting: PCB only: 40-60°C/W; With heatsink: 10-20°C/W; Busbar mount: 5-10°C/W. Temperature rise: ΔT = P × θja. With busbar mount: 4W × 5°C/W = 20°C rise. Design guidelines: Keep Tj < 150°C (Tambient + rise < 150°C); Use thermal interface material (TIM) for busbar mounting; Ensure adequate copper area on PCB; Consider airflow for convection cooling; Monitor temperature with integrated sensor. Example: At 85°C ambient with 20°C rise, Tj = 105°C, well within limits. At 125°C ambient, margin is reduced to 25°C.
faqs.decisionGuide: Calculate power dissipation and temperature rise; use busbar mounting for best thermal performance; keep Tj < 150°C.

faqs.question: Can Allegro sensors measure both charging and discharging currents?
faqs.answer: Yes, Allegro current sensors are bidirectional and measure both charging and discharging currents. The output is centered at Vcc/2 (2.5V for 5V supply), with positive currents increasing voltage and negative currents decreasing voltage. For ±200A range with 10mV/A sensitivity: Full scale positive (+200A): Vout = 2.5V + (200 × 0.01) = 4.5V; Zero current (0A): Vout = 2.5V; Full scale negative (-200A): Vout = 2.5V - (200 × 0.01) = 0.5V. This bidirectional capability is essential for EV battery management to monitor both charging (regenerative braking, plug-in charging) and discharging (traction, accessory loads). The ratiometric output ensures accurate bidirectional measurement even with supply voltage variations.
faqs.decisionGuide: Allegro sensors are inherently bidirectional; output centered at Vcc/2; essential for EV charge/discharge monitoring.
